title: Coin Change
code: public int CoinChange(int[] coins, int amount)

dp.label: dp[a] = min coins to make amount a

frames.caption: Base case: dp[0] = 0 (no coins needed for amount 0). All other amounts start at the sentinel ∞ = amount + 1. Fill dp left to right, smallest amount first.
frames.note: State: dp[a] = the fewest coins that sum to amount a. dp[0] = 0 (zero coins make 0). Every other cell starts at the sentinel amount+1 — a value larger than any real answer (at most `amount` 1-coins), so Math.Min never picks an unreachable amount, and a cell still holding the sentinel at the end means "impossible". Capacity runs ASCENDING because coins are unbounded: a just-updated dp[a-c] can be reused within the same sweep, which is exactly what "use a coin any number of times" means.

frames.caption: a=1: try coins 1 → min(dp[0]+1=0+1=1) = 1, so dp[1] = 1.
frames.note: For amount a we try every coin c that fits (c <= a) and look BACK at dp[a-c] — the already-solved subproblem of "make the remainder" — then add 1 for the coin c itself. Taking the min over all coins keeps only the cheapest way to land on a. Because every dp[a-c] with a-c < a was filled on an earlier (smaller) amount, each transition reads finished cells in O(1) — that look-back-at-a-smaller-amount is the whole DP.

frames.caption: a=2: try coins 1 → min(dp[1]+1=1+1=2) = 2, so dp[2] = 2.
frames.note: For amount a we try every coin c that fits (c <= a) and look BACK at dp[a-c] — the already-solved subproblem of "make the remainder" — then add 1 for the coin c itself. Taking the min over all coins keeps only the cheapest way to land on a. Because every dp[a-c] with a-c < a was filled on an earlier (smaller) amount, each transition reads finished cells in O(1) — that look-back-at-a-smaller-amount is the whole DP.

frames.caption: a=3: try coins 1, 3 → min(dp[2]+1=2+1=3, dp[0]+1=0+1=1) = 1, so dp[3] = 1.
frames.note: For amount a we try every coin c that fits (c <= a) and look BACK at dp[a-c] — the already-solved subproblem of "make the remainder" — then add 1 for the coin c itself. Taking the min over all coins keeps only the cheapest way to land on a. Because every dp[a-c] with a-c < a was filled on an earlier (smaller) amount, each transition reads finished cells in O(1) — that look-back-at-a-smaller-amount is the whole DP.

frames.caption: a=4: try coins 1, 3, 4 → min(dp[3]+1=1+1=2, dp[1]+1=1+1=2, dp[0]+1=0+1=1) = 1, so dp[4] = 1.
frames.note: For amount a we try every coin c that fits (c <= a) and look BACK at dp[a-c] — the already-solved subproblem of "make the remainder" — then add 1 for the coin c itself. Taking the min over all coins keeps only the cheapest way to land on a. Because every dp[a-c] with a-c < a was filled on an earlier (smaller) amount, each transition reads finished cells in O(1) — that look-back-at-a-smaller-amount is the whole DP.

frames.caption: a=5: try coins 1, 3, 4 → min(dp[4]+1=1+1=2, dp[2]+1=2+1=3, dp[1]+1=1+1=2) = 2, so dp[5] = 2.
frames.note: For amount a we try every coin c that fits (c <= a) and look BACK at dp[a-c] — the already-solved subproblem of "make the remainder" — then add 1 for the coin c itself. Taking the min over all coins keeps only the cheapest way to land on a. Because every dp[a-c] with a-c < a was filled on an earlier (smaller) amount, each transition reads finished cells in O(1) — that look-back-at-a-smaller-amount is the whole DP.

frames.caption: a=6: try coins 1, 3, 4 → min(dp[5]+1=2+1=3, dp[3]+1=1+1=2, dp[2]+1=2+1=3) = 2, so dp[6] = 2.
frames.note: For amount a we try every coin c that fits (c <= a) and look BACK at dp[a-c] — the already-solved subproblem of "make the remainder" — then add 1 for the coin c itself. Taking the min over all coins keeps only the cheapest way to land on a. Because every dp[a-c] with a-c < a was filled on an earlier (smaller) amount, each transition reads finished cells in O(1) — that look-back-at-a-smaller-amount is the whole DP.

frames.caption: Answer is dp[6] = 2 — the fewest coins to make 6 from [1, 3, 4] is 2 (3 + 3).
frames.note: dp[amount] is the answer over the full target. It cost O(amount × coins) time and O(amount) space — pseudo-polynomial: polynomial in the numeric value of amount, not its bit length. This is why DP beats greedy here: greedy on [1,3,4] for 6 grabs 4+1+1 = 3 coins, but the table proves 3+3 = 2 is better by comparing every coin against an already-optimal smaller amount. If dp[amount] had stayed at the sentinel (> amount) we would return -1.
